msg: Analyze tradeoffs between SQLite WAL mode vs journal mode for concurrent read-heavy write-light workloads.
stdout:
{
  "runId": "0d4964c4-4066-4ac8-9a6f-2893a114054e",
  "status": "ok",
  "summary": "completed",
  "result": {
    "payloads": [
      {
        "text": "## SQLite: WAL vs Rollback Journal — Concurrent Read-Heavy, Write-Light\n\n### WAL wins here. Here's why:\n\n**Concurrency**\n- **WAL**: Readers don't block writers, writers don't block readers. Multiple concurrent readers proceed in parallel.\n- **Journal**: A write lock blocks all readers. Even short writes cause read stalls.\n\nFor read-heavy workloads, this alone is usually decisive.\n\n**Read performance**\n- **WAL**: Reads hit the main DB + WAL file. As long as WAL doesn't grow huge, reads are fast and uncontested.\n- **Journal**: Reads are simple (single file), but frequently blocked by write locks.\n\n**Write performance**\n- **WAL**: Writes append to WAL sequentially (fast). Checkpoint merges WAL→DB periodically.\n- **Journal**: Each write copies original pages to journal first (random I/O), then modifies DB in-place. Slightly more I/O per write.\n\n### Where Journal mode still makes sense\n\n| Factor | Favors Journal |\n|---|---|\n| **Network/shared filesystems** | WAL requires shared-memory (`-shm` file), breaks on NFS/SMB |\n| **Single-process simplicity** | No checkpoint tuning needed |\n| **Large transactions** | WAL file can grow unbounded until checkpoint; journal is bounded by transaction size |\n| **Read-after-write from same connection** | Both fine, but WAL checkpoint lag can surprise if you query from a *different* connection expecting immediate visibility |\n| **Disk space** | WAL keeps both DB + WAL + shm; journal only exists during writes |\n\n### WAL gotchas to manage\n\n- **Checkpoint policy**: Auto-checkpoint defaults to 1000 pages. For write-light workloads this is fine, but monitor WAL file size.\n- **`-wal` and `-shm` files**: Must stay alongside the DB. Backup tools that copy only the `.db` file will lose data.\n- **Single writer still**: WAL doesn't enable concurrent *writes* — just concurrent reads alongside a single writer.\n- **`BEGIN IMMEDIATE`/`EXCLUSIVE`**: Still needed if you want to guarantee a write slot without SQLITE_BUSY retries.\n\n### Recommendation\n\nFor your profile (read-heavy, write-light, presumably single-host):\n\n```sql\nPRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;\nPRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL;  -- safe with WAL, faster than FULL\nPRAGMA wal_autocheckpoint=1000;  -- default, usually fine\n```\n\n`synchronous=NORMAL` with WAL gives durability against app crashes (not OS crashes). If you need OS-crash durability, keep `FULL` — the perf cost is modest for write-light loads.",
        "mediaUrl": null
      }
    ],
